lesson.menu_desc: Tell the growth story to the board: set the backdrop, build to a turning point with narrative tenses, switch to the dramatic present for the key moment, signpost every step, and land one clear takeaway — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 11 — Presenting to the Board — Slides & Stories
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 11 — Presenting to the Board

listenings.text: Okay, seven minutes with the board, and every second counts. I will not bury the headline this time. I open with the one number that matters, revenue up nineteen percent, and then I walk them through how we got there. Backdrop first: the market had been sliding for two quarters while we were quietly rebuilding the pipeline. That sets up the turning point, the new route we launched in March. I want them to feel that moment, so I will switch to the present: so we go live, and within a week the orders double. Then I drill down into two numbers, no more, because boards drown in detail. I signpost every move so nobody gets lost: start with the headline, walk through the story, close with the takeaway. And the takeaway is one line: this model scales to three more regions. Keep the storyline tight, make it compelling, land that one point. Right. Let me run it once more.

Hi Victor, it is Bianca, your presentation coach. Three quick reminders before you face the board. First, signpost everything. Tell them where you are going, let me start with the headline, then walk you through the detail, and they will follow you anywhere. A board that knows the map never gets lost. Second, tell it as a story, not a data dump. Use the backdrop to set the scene, build to one clear turning point, and switch to the present for the moment you want them to feel: so we go live, and the orders double. That is what makes a presentation compelling. Third, do not end on a slide; end on a takeaway. Boards remember one line, not twenty charts, so decide the single sentence you want them repeating in the hallway, and land it hard. Signpost, build the story, land the takeaway. You have got this, Victor. Go tell it well.
